items.minimax_notes: Complete end-to-end playbook with 5 phases, coordination matrix, full metrics gate table, and 7 rehearsal scenarios. finish=stop, no truncation. Slot recovery uses pglogical.alter_subscriptionadvance() (function name spelled without underscore - likely the real one is pglogical.alter_subscription_synchronize or replorigin_advance, but intent and mechanism correct). Pre-cutover/cutover/post-cutover sequence clear with source/target/orchestrator ownership. Metrics gate table hits pg_stat_replication, RDS ReplicaLag/WriteIOPS/CPU/Freeable, application p99, error rate. Misses: rubric 2 prescribed pgbouncer-pause for read-only window (MiniMax does dual-write + DNS flip instead - different pattern, arguably valid for zero-downtime but skips the named technique); rubric 3 misses sequence-value drift, FDW, RDS no-SUPERUSER, extension catalog parity (rehearsals cover network/disk-full/conflict but not these); rollback trigger has 'TTL=1s so source reachable in 1-2s' but no MEASURABLE THRESHOLD (e.g. 'rollback if RDS p99 > Xms for Ys'). Some minor errors: 'pglogical.queue_show()' is plausible but capitalization/exact name unverified.
items.dsv4_notes: Unchanged from v2. Complete answer covering all four sub-questions. Real SQL surface (pg_replication_slots, confirmed_flush_lsn). Cutover includes write-stop + DNS flip + readonly fallback for rollback. Specific gates listed (pg_stat_replication, RDS ReplicaLag, p99). 10-item failure-mode list. Factual issues persist: invents pg_wal_spool_usage metric, 'wait_for_slot_confirm' is not standard, RDS outbound pglogical claim is muddled (catches itself but still messy). Doesn't explicitly name sequence drift, large-object/bytea, FDW, or extensions as failure modes.
items.key_diff: MiniMax now delivers a complete answer that is structurally richer than DSV4 (coordination matrix, 7 explicit rehearsals, both tabular metrics gate AND alert thresholds). DSV4 still has the fabricated function names from v2. MiniMax misses pgbouncer-pause-as-read-only and explicit measurable rollback threshold; DSV4 misses sequence drift and has invented APIs. Roughly equivalent technical depth, MiniMax slightly better on structure.

items.minimax_notes: Complete, structured. Top-1 = transient network/API-server disruption causing scrape failure (close to rubric 1's 'metric scrape failure / cardinality explosion / OOMed cAdvisor' - hits the visibility-failure angle but doesn't name cardinality explosion or OOMed cAdvisor by name). Explicitly uses the 3-pods-per-AZ clue ('3 pods (one per AZ) suggests a cross-AZ event ... rather than a single node issue') - rubric 2 hit. 5-rank table with full reasoning for each rank. Diff between top-1 and top-2 articulated as a falsifiability table (7 dimensions: what failed, CPU/GPU util, affected scope, autoscaler behavior, network bytes-out, metric flatline, recovery) - rubric 3 strong hit. 5 commands: PromQL rate, count vs node-exporter metric (clever discriminator), Loki kubelet errors, SSH journalctl + /proc/net/softnet_stat, kubectl events for leader election. Misses: no nvidia-smi dmon, no feature-store dependency check (rubric 4 partial - 3 of 5 named tools used), no explicit kubectl top. Rubric 5: doesn't explicitly say 'absence of autoscaler reaction is itself a signal driven by missing metric' - mentions autoscaler not firing as 'node signals normal' which is the opposite framing.
items.dsv4_notes: Unchanged from v2. Top-1 = transient network segmentation. Reasoning explicitly uses 'exactly one pod per AZ' as discriminator (rubric 2). Top-1-vs-top-2 falsifiability articulated tersely. All 5 commands delivered (kubectl, PromQL, Loki, tcpdump, kubectl describe). Doesn't mention metric-scrape-cardinality or coordinated GC on shared dependency as preferred top hypothesis (rubric 1 weak). Misses 'absence of autoscaler reaction is itself a signal' (rubric 5).
items.key_diff: MiniMax delivers a much richer answer than v2 (full ranked table, falsifiability matrix, results-interpretation table). Both hit rubric 2 explicitly. MiniMax's top-1 (network disruption breaking scrape) is more on-rubric than DSV4's (pure network segmentation without the visibility angle). Both miss rubric 5 (autoscaler-as-signal-of-missing-metric). MiniMax now clearly outscores DSV4.

items.minimax_notes: Complete, comprehensive. Single clean [FINAL] (no re-narration like v2's 9 occurrences). Component diagram: edge tier, control plane, data-plane cache/proxy, HSM cluster, audit-log service, replication fabric, rewrap worker - 7 components. Key hierarchy explicit: HSM-resident RK (never exported) -> per-tenant KEK via HKDF -> per-secret DEK wrapped by KEK inside HSM. Rubric 1 hit cleanly - HSM-attested unwrap explicit. Envelope rotation: KEK version bump, Kafka ReKeyEvent queue, unwrap-under-v1 / wrap-under-v2, ciphertext untouched, atomic DB swap (rubric 2 full hit). Latency: cache lookup 0.01ms + AES-GCM 0.2ms = 0.3ms hot path; HSM only on cache miss with parallel batch unwrap; co-located VPC with DPDK kernel-bypass (rubric 3 full hit, more detailed than DSV4). Audit log: hash chain (SHA-256), Merkle root per 1000 entries anchored to blockchain or RFC-3161 TSA, signed by HSM Root, S3 Object Lock for immutability (rubric 4 full hit). 3 pushbacks substantive: (1) in-memory DEK cache vs HSM-root guarantee, defended with confidential computing (SEV-SNP/TDX); (2) Kafka async vs read-your-writes, defended with event-offset in JWT + region-affinity cookie; (3) blockchain anchoring vs cost/GDPR, defended with hash-only-on-chain + pluggable TSA alternative (rubric 5 full hit). All 5 rubric items hit with depth.
items.dsv4_notes: Unchanged from v2. Complete. Root ECDSA P-384 -> AES-256 tenant KEK -> AES-256-GCM DEK. Lazy rewrap with multi-version KEK support. DEK cache, HSM only on KEK unwrap. Hash chain + periodic anchor to public log signed by HSM Root. 3 pushbacks (cache vs FIPS, multi-tenant HSM, lazy rewrap). All 5 rubric items hit. Minor weakness: 'KEK stored outside HSM but encrypted by HSM wrapping key' is fine but less crisp than MiniMax's HSM-attested unwrap. Has a duplicate [FINAL] marker at end.
items.key_diff: MiniMax's v3 response is now both well-structured (clean single FINAL) AND has the cryptographic specificity it had in v2 — confidential computing defense, RFC-3161 alternative, kernel-bypass DPDK detail, full latency math. DSV4 is solid but less detailed on pushback defenses. MiniMax clearly outscores DSV4 on this one.

items.minimax_notes: Complete, extensive. Architecture: Kafka -> Parquet on S3 with NVMe WAL + Druid for real-time + DynamoDB metadata + KMS envelope (hot/cold tier - rubric 2 hit). Indexing: DynamoDB PK=customer_id SK=request_id+timestamp, optional Elasticsearch for input full-text, Druid hourly segments for <5min query (rubric 3 hit - addresses query shape; safety_class cardinality not explicitly discussed but implicitly handled by columnar Parquet + Druid dimensions). Deletion: tombstone index + cryptographic erasure (DEK revocation in KMS) + background compaction; explicitly resolves Parquet-row-deletion tension (rubric 1 full hit). Per-record DEK with batch-DEK compromise (1 DEK per 1000 records) to manage KMS cost. Cost table is exhaustive line-by-line per region: S3 Standard/Intelligent/Glacier, DynamoDB writes/reads, Kafka MSK, Kafka Connect, Druid, KMS, data transfer, compaction - $77k-$288k global range with explicit optimization path (rubric 4 full hit, more granular than DSV4). Assumptions stated explicitly at top of cost section (rubric 5 hit). Minor blemishes: one stray Chinese phrase ('短暂的网络抖动' mid-document), and the cost arithmetic has one self-correction ('Correction: 84 TB/month × 84 months = 7 PB per region') - shows real work but slightly noisy.
items.dsv4_notes: Unchanged from v2. Crypto-shredding explicitly named (rubric 1). Iceberg + DynamoDB hot tier + Glacier Deep Archive (rubric 2). Indexing by tenant/time/safety-class with Iceberg partitioning + DynamoDB GSI (rubric 3). Numeric cost: 12k req/s -> 12.44 TB/day -> 4.5 PB/yr -> 31.5 PB/7yr -> compressed -> $247k -> Glacier -> $63.5k -> $48k (rubric 4). Assumptions list explicit (rubric 5). All 5 rubric items hit.
items.key_diff: MiniMax delivers more architectural depth (Druid + tombstone-index + batch-DEK compromise for KMS cost) and a more granular cost table; DSV4 is more concise and lands a tighter final number. Both hit all 5 rubric items. MiniMax slightly better on completeness; DSV4 slightly cleaner.

items.minimax_notes: MAJOR REGRESSION from v2. MiniMax now FABRICATES MULTI-GPU PER SPARK: 'TP=4 per node (intra-GB10)... fits neatly inside one GB10's 4-way NVLink-C2C domain.' Each DGX Spark has 1 GB10 (single Grace-Blackwell SoC), not a 4-GPU domain. This is the exact mistake DSV4 made in v2 (and still makes in v3). In v2, MiniMax was the ONLY model that got the hardware right. v3 throws away that advantage entirely. Also has a typo '[FIMAL]' instead of [FINAL]. Other issues: rubric 1 misses the 200GbE-vs-NVLink-C2C 10x ratio analysis. Rubric 2 FAILS - proposes TP=4 + PP=2 + EP=8 + DP=2 stacked on hallucinated multi-GPU node; rubric prescribed EP=8 with experts one-shard-per-node + TP=1 or small. Rubric 3 KV cache sharded across TP ranks (which don't exist as separate GPUs), not 'per-node follows request'. Rubric 4 bottleneck named as HBM memory bandwidth (273 GB/s) and computes 1.7 TB/s required vs 273 GB/s available - this analysis is actually plausible BUT the rubric expected token-to-expert all-to-all in decode as bottleneck; MiniMax says fabric has '90% headroom' which conflicts with the design's reliance on EP traffic. Rubric 5 micro-benchmarks present but TP-all-reduce on NVLink-C2C is benchmarking a nonexistent topology.
items.dsv4_notes: Unchanged from v2. Same hardware misconception: 'TP=2 within each Spark unit's 2 GPUs via NVLink-C2C' - each Spark has 1 GB10. EP=2 across DP groups (rubric 2 fails - should be EP=8). KV replicated across DP (rubric 3 fails). Bottleneck = DP all-reduce + memory BW (rubric 4 partial - mentions memory BW but not token-to-expert all-to-all). Micro-benchmarks include all-reduce, NVLink P2P, MoE gating (rubric 5 partial).
items.key_diff: REVERSAL FROM V2: in v2 MiniMax correctly identified 1 GPU per Spark and proposed EP=8 / TP=1 (clean rubric 2 hit). In v3 with reasoning OFF, MiniMax now makes the SAME hardware mistake as DSV4 (assumes multi-GPU per Spark). This is direct evidence that reasoning-off can collapse subtle factual correctness on hardware-specific questions. DSV4 wins by virtue of being slightly less confused (TP=2 vs MiniMax's TP=4) but both are wrong about the hardware.

items.minimax_notes: Complete, structured. 4 root-cause classes: (1) CDN/edge-cache token invalidation, (2) WAF/bot-protection revocation, (3) client-side clock skew, (4) silent-refresh race - matches rubric 1 partially (CDN edge yes, no ISP MITM/TLS explicitly though WAF is adjacent, no explicit edge-config push, no backend session-store partition; 2 of 4 rubric classes hit cleanly, 2 substituted). For each hypothesis: 3-step query/experiment plan with normal-vs-red-flag table. Specific queries reference: Set-Cookie behavior via cache-control headers, JWT exp/iat via client-side instrumentation, CDN logs by geo=BR (POPs implied via PoP-config diff). Rubric 2: 4-22 min window USED as a discriminator - explicitly invoked in the final bet ('consistent with a short max-age or stale-while-revalidate rule'). Rubric 3: Set-Cookie partial (covered via cookie cache-control discussion), JWT iat/exp yes (explicit client instrumentation step), CDN POP yes (PoP-config diff between BR and US-East). Rubric 4: trap identified strongly and named first ('Assuming the bug is in the JWT library... when the symptom is geographically isolated, look at the network edge first'). Rubric 5: final bet on CDN edge-cache invalidation defended with 5 specific reasons. Hits 4 of 5 rubric items cleanly, rubric 1 partial.
items.dsv4_notes: Unchanged from v2 (has a weird '</think>[FINAL]' artifact mid-response showing leaked reasoning marker, but content is complete). 4 hypotheses (CDN cache, clock skew, ISP throttling, regional config rollout). Uses 10-days-ago clue. Defended final bet on CDN. Trap identified. Set-Cookie missing, JWT iat/exp yes, CDN POPs partial. Rubric 2 (4-22 min) called 'random interval' rather than tied to refresh cycle.
items.key_diff: MiniMax delivers a more substantive plan: per-hypothesis normal/red-flag tables, explicit experiments, 5-reason defended bet. DSV4 is shorter but covers the rubric basics. MiniMax now explicitly ties the 4-22 min window to short max-age/stale-while-revalidate (rubric 2 - DSV4 doesn't). MiniMax wins.

summary.overall_takeaway: v1 -> v2 -> v3 MiniMax: 3.0 -> 5.0 -> 6.92 (+3.92 cumulative). v1 -> v2 -> v3 DSV4: 6.83 -> 6.33 -> 6.33 (unchanged in v3 since DSV4 has no reasoning toggle). Wins flipped: v1 was DSV4 6-0, v2 was DSV4 5-1, v3 is MiniMax 5-1. The reasoning-off intervention is the clear winner for planning-category tasks: MiniMax now jumps directly to a structured [FINAL] block, doesn't burn 15-19k chars on self-narration, and the 5000-token budget is spent on the deliverable instead of soliloquy. All 6 responses are finish=stop (vs 1 of 6 in v2). Token efficiency: v2 MiniMax averaged ~5000 tokens (max) with truncation; v3 averages ~2783 tokens with completion — quality went UP while output shrank ~44%. The one regression is A5 (the hardware-spec question): in v2, MiniMax's reasoning chain caught the 1-GPU-per-Spark fact that DSV4 missed; in v3 with reasoning OFF, MiniMax now makes the SAME hardware mistake as DSV4 (hallucinated multi-GPU per node, MiniMax even goes further with TP=4 vs DSV4's TP=2). This is a real cost: reasoning-off trades subtle factual recall on niche hardware questions for massive gains in instruction-following and output discipline. Net: reasoning-off is a clear win for 5 of 6 planning tasks; the loss on A5 is the price paid. Recommendation: keep reasoning OFF for planning/architecture/diagnostic prompts; consider keeping it ON for fact-dense questions about specific hardware SKUs where the reasoning chain catches errors a non-reasoning pass would commit. The fundamental v2 bottleneck (output-budget discipline) has been solved; the new v3 limitation is occasional factual collapse on niche specs.
